content:
# Genesis Chapter 14 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 8r–8v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_014_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_014_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 9–10 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** And it came to pass in the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim,

**2** that these kings made war with Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, that is, Zoar.

**3** All these joined together in the Valley of Siddim, that is, the Salt Sea.

**4** Twelve years they had served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled.

**5** And in the fourteenth year Chedorlaomer came, and the kings who were with him, and they struck the Rephaim in Ashteroth-karnaim, and the Zuzim in Ham, and the Emim in Shaveh-kiriathaim,

**6** and the Horites in their hill country of Seir as far as El-paran, which is by the wilderness.

**7** Then they turned back and came to En-mishpat, that is, Kadesh, and they struck all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites who were dwelling in Hazazon-tamar.

**8** Then the king of Sodom, the king of Gomorrah, the king of Admah, the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, that is, Zoar, went out and they drew up for battle against them in the Valley of Siddim:

**9** against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of Goiim, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar — four kings against five.

**10** Now the Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits, and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and they fell there. And those who remained fled to the hill country.

**11** And the victors took all the possessions of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their provisions, and they went their way.

**12** They also took Lot, the son of Abram's brother, and his possessions, and departed, for he was dwelling in Sodom.

**13** Then one who had escaped came and told Abram the Hebrew. Now he was dwelling by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite, brother of Eshcol and brother of Aner. They were allies of Abram.

**14** When Abram heard that his kinsman had been taken captive, he led out his trained men, born in his household — three hundred and eighteen — and pursued as far as Dan.

**15** And he divided against them by night, he and his servants, and struck them and pursued them as far as Hobah, which is north of Damascus.

**16** And he brought back all the possessions. He also brought back his kinsman Lot and his possessions, and the women and the people.

**17** After his return from the defeat of Chedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him at the Valley of Shaveh, that is, the King's Valley.

**18** And Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine. Now he was priest of God Most High.

**19** And he blessed him and said, "Blessed be Abram by God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth;

**20** and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand." And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.

**21** And the king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the persons, and take the possessions for yourself."

**22** But Abram said to the king of Sodom, "I have lifted my hand to the LORD, God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth,

**23** that I will not take a thread or a sandal strap or anything that is yours, lest you should say, 'I have made Abram rich.'

**24** I will take nothing but what the young men have eaten, and the share of the men who went with me — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. Let them take their share."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 14 spans Folios 8r–8v.

   **Folio 8r** (GEN_014_page_001.jpg): This folio carries the end of Genesis 13 (visible in the right-hand columns) and the opening of Genesis 14 beginning at verse 1. The three-column Masoretic layout is clear. The characteristic list-structure of verses 1–2 is visible with the four eastern kings set against the five kings of the plain; the names אַמְרָפֶל מֶלֶךְ שִׁנְעָר (Amraphel king of Shinar), אַרְיוֹךְ מֶלֶךְ אֶלָּסָר (Arioch king of Ellasar), כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר מֶלֶךְ עֵילָם (Chedorlaomer king of Elam), and תִּדְעָל מֶלֶךְ גּוֹיִם (Tidal king of Goiim) are legible in the opening verses. The Valley of Siddim (עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים) and its identification with the Salt Sea (יָם הַמֶּלַח) in verse 3 are visible. The campaigns against the Rephaim, Zuzim, Emim, and Horites (vv. 5–6) and the battle narrative (vv. 8–10) continue through the column. The critical notice of verse 12 — the taking of Lot — is legible, and the identification of Abram in verse 13 as הָעִבְרִי (the Hebrew) is visible. The call of Abram's trained men (חֲנִיכָיו), the number שְׁמֹנֶה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת (318), and the pursuit to Dan are readable through the mid-column area. The folio continues through approximately verse 16.

   **Folio 8v** (GEN_014_page_002.jpg): This folio opens with the conclusion of the battle narrative and carries the theologically climactic Melchizedek episode (vv. 17–20) in the central columns. The name מַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם (Melchizedek king of Salem) is legible in verse 18 alongside the identification as כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן (priest of God Most High). The dual title אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ (God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth) is visible in both verse 19 (the blessing) and verse 22 (Abram's oath). The tenth (מַעֲשֵׂר) in verse 20 is legible. The dialogue with the king of Sodom (vv. 21–24) concludes the chapter, and the Masoretic section markers at the end of the pericope are visible. The folio continues into the opening of Genesis 15.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The chapter marker "CAP. XIV" (Γ CAP. XIIII) is visible at the left margin of page 9 (GEN_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg). The LXX Genesis 14 text is legible in the three-column uncial format, covering the war narrative through the first part of the Melchizedek episode. Page 10 (GEN_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder of the Melchizedek episode and the dialogue with the king of Sodom, concluding with the transition to Genesis 15 (CAP. XV marker visible at the right margin). The LXX text agrees substantially with the MT across the chapter, with the principal variants noted in the table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. The Fourfold List of Eastern Kings (vv. 1–2) — Political Geography**

The chapter opens with a striking literary structure: four eastern kings are set against five kings of the plain in a 4-vs-5 confrontation that verse 9 makes explicit. The names of the eastern kings — אַמְרָפֶל (Amraphel), אַרְיוֹךְ (Arioch), כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר (Chedorlaomer), and תִּדְעָל (Tidal) — have been the subject of extensive historical identification attempts. Amraphel of Shinar was once identified with Hammurabi of Babylon, but this equation has been abandoned by scholars: the phonological correspondence is inadequate, and no extrabiblical attestation links Amraphel to Hammurabi. Chedorlaomer of Elam similarly resists secure extrabiblical identification. Tidal "king of Goiim" (גּוֹיִם, "nations") may reflect a title for a ruler of mixed or nomadic peoples rather than a specific geographic kingdom.

The five kings of the plain correspond to the cities of the pentapolis later associated with the destruction narrative of Genesis 18–19: Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela/Zoar. The repeated explanatory glosses — "that is, the Salt Sea" (v. 3), "that is, Kadesh" (v. 7), "that is, Zoar" (vv. 2, 8), "that is, the King's Valley" (v. 17) — are a distinctive feature of the chapter's style, marking it as a composition attentive to the gap between the names current in the narrative period and the names by which readers know these places. The formal translation preserves all these glosses without bracketing or footnoting them.

The LXX Vaticanus renders the geographic and personal names with standard Greek transliterations: Αμαρφαλ βασιλεὺς Σεννααρ (Amarphal king of Sennaar), Αριωχ βασιλεὺς Ελλασαρ (Arioch king of Ellassar), Χοδολλογομορ βασιλεὺς Αιλαμ (Chodollogomor king of Ailam), and Θαργαλ βασιλεὺς ἐθνῶν (Thargal king of nations). The LXX's "king of nations" (βασιλεὺς ἐθνῶν) for מֶלֶךְ גּוֹיִם (melek goyyim) is a meaningful translation — rendering the Hebrew goyyim ("nations, peoples, Gentiles") with its Greek equivalent ἔθνη (ethne), precisely as the MT requires. The formal translation renders "Tidal king of Goiim" using the Hebrew proper name, as "Goiim" is not merely a common noun in this context but appears to function as a proper designation.

**2. עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים — "The Valley of Siddim" (v. 3)**

The Valley of Siddim (עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים) is identified in verse 3 with יָם הַמֶּלַח (yam hamelach, "the Salt Sea") — the Dead Sea. This identification suggests the valley was subsequently submerged, which is consistent with the geology of the region: the Dead Sea and its surrounding basin have undergone significant changes in water level and extent. The word שִׂדִּים (siddim) is of uncertain etymology; it may be related to שָׂדֶה (sadeh, "field") or to an otherwise unattested Semitic root. The formal translation follows the standard practice of rendering it as a proper name throughout.

The tar pits of verse 10 — בְּאֵרֹת בְּאֵרֹת חֵמָר (be'erot be'erot chemar, "pits, pits of tar" — a double plural emphasizing the numerous nature of the hazard) — are consistent with the geological character of the region, where bitumen seeps are attested in antiquity (Josephus calls the Dead Sea "Lake Asphaltitis"). The formal translation renders "full of tar pits" — collapsing the doubled plural construction into the natural English idiom while preserving the material substance (חֵמָר, chemar, "bitumen, tar").

**3. שְׁמֹנֶה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת — "Three Hundred and Eighteen" (v. 14)**

The number of Abram's trained men — שְׁמֹנֶה עָשָׂר וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת (shemoneh asar ushloshtayim, 318) — is given with the characteristic Hebrew grammatical order: the units first (eighteen) followed by the hundreds (three hundred), rendered in English as 318. The Masoretic Codex is clear on this number. The LXX reads τριακοσίους δέκα καὶ ὀκτώ (triakosioi deka kai okto, 318) — in full agreement with the MT.

The number 318 is a hapax in the sense that it is the only time this specific figure appears in the biblical text. In later rabbinic tradition (Genesis Rabbah 43:2), Eliezer alone was reckoned equivalent to 318 by gematria (the numerical value of the name אֱלִיעֶזֶר = 318). The Epistle of Barnabas (9:8) also interprets this number christologically. These later interpretations are noted for their historical interest but do not affect the translation, which renders the number straightforwardly as "three hundred and eighteen."

The word חֲנִיכִים (chanichim, "trained men") is a hapax legomenon — it appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. The root is debated: it may be related to חֲנַךְ (chanak, "to initiate, to dedicate, to train") from which חֲנֻכָּה (Hanukkah, "dedication, consecration") is derived, suggesting these are men specially initiated or trained. Alternatively, the word may be cognate with Egyptian ḥnkw (a term for retainers or household fighters). The context — men "born in his household" (יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ) who are mustered for military pursuit — establishes them as domestic retainers who serve as a private military force. The formal translation renders "trained men" as the most transparent English equivalent, with the understanding that the word's hapax character means certainty is not available.

The LXX renders τοὺς ἰδίους οἰκογενεῖς αὐτοῦ (tous idious oikogeneis autou, "his own house-born men") — capturing the "born in his household" element but not separately rendering chanichim as a distinct military designation. The MT's chanichim is more specific; the formal translation preserves it with "trained men."

**4. הָעִבְרִי — "the Hebrew" (v. 13)**

The phrase אַבְרָם הָעִבְרִי (Avram ha-ivri, "Abram the Hebrew") in verse 13 is the first use of the gentilicial designation עִבְרִי (ivri, "Hebrew") in the Hebrew Bible. Its etymology is contested. Three main theories have been proposed:

(a) **The Eber theory:** עִבְרִי (ivri) is derived from עֵבֶר (Eber), the ancestor of Abram in the Table of Nations (Genesis 10:21, 24–25; 11:14–17). On this reading, "the Hebrew" means "a descendant of Eber."

(b) **The crossing theory:** עִבְרִי (ivri) is derived from the root עָבַר (avar, "to cross over, to pass through"). On this reading, "the Hebrew" means "the one who crossed over" — the one who came from across the Euphrates River (כִּי מֵעֵבֶר הַנָּהָר עֲבוֹתֵיכֶם, Joshua 24:3: "from beyond the River"). This would designate Abram as the one who crossed the great river to enter Canaan, an outsider by geographic origin.

(c) **The Habiru theory:** Some scholars have connected עִבְרִי with the Akkadian term Ḫapiru/Ḫabiru, found in Late Bronze Age texts from Mari, Nuzi, and the Amarna letters, designating a class of socially marginal, semi-nomadic, often mercenary individuals who moved across national borders outside the regular social structures. On this reading, "the Hebrew" would carry a social-class or socioeconomic valence as much as an ethnic one.

The formal translation renders "Abram the Hebrew" — using the standard English term that has designated this people throughout the history of English Bible translation, without resolving the etymological debate. The LXX renders Αβραμ τῷ περάτῃ (Abram to Peratē, "Abram the crosser" or "Abram the one from across") — a Greek rendering that appears to interpret עִבְרִי through the "crossing over" etymology (from περάω, "to cross over"). This is a significant LXX exegetical decision: the LXX translator understood the term etymologically as a geographic designator ("the one from across") rather than a genealogical one. The formal translation preserves the conventional "Hebrew" rather than adopting the LXX's etymological interpretation.

**5. מַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם — Melchizedek King of Salem (v. 18)**

The figure of מַלְכִּי-צֶדֶק (Malki-tsedeq, "Melchizedek") is the most theologically charged individual in Genesis 14, and one of the most theologically significant figures in the entire Hebrew Bible. He appears suddenly, without genealogy or prior introduction, and disappears after three verses, never to appear again in the Pentateuch. His name is a compound: מֶלֶךְ (melek, "king") + צֶדֶק (tsedeq, "righteousness, rightness, justice") = "king of righteousness" or "my king is righteousness." This naming convention is typical of ancient Semitic theophoric or virtue names.

He is identified as:
- מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם (melek shalem, "king of Salem") — universally identified with Jerusalem (שָׁלֵם = the archaic name for Jerusalem; cf. Psalm 76:2: "His dwelling place is in Salem"). The word שָׁלֵם (shalem) is related to שָׁלוֹם (shalom, "peace, wholeness"), giving the title a resonance of "king of peace" (so Hebrews 7:2). The formal translation preserves "Salem" as the proper place name.
- כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן (kohen le'el elyon, "priest of God Most High") — the combination of royal and priestly offices in a single figure is extraordinary. No other figure in the Patriarchal narratives simultaneously holds both offices. This combination will be explicitly invoked in Psalm 110:4 ("You are a priest forever, according to the order of Melchizedek") in an oracle addressed to the Davidic king, and the Epistle to the Hebrews (chapters 5–7) develops an extended typological argument on the basis of this combination.

The name is rendered "Melchizedek" throughout — the established English form, following the standard transliteration tradition.

The LXX renders: Μελχισεδεκ βασιλεὺς Σαλημ (Melchisedek king of Salem) — in full agreement with the MT on the name and title. The priestly identification κὶ ἱερεὺς τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ὑψίστου (hoti hiereus tou theou tou hypsistou, "for he was priest of God the Most High") agrees with the MT in rendering אֵל עֶלְיוֹן as θεός ὕψιστος ("God Most High").

**6. אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ — "God Most High, Possessor of Heaven and Earth" (vv. 19, 22)**

The divine epithet אֵל עֶלְיוֹן (El Elyon) is used four times in this chapter (vv. 18, 19, 20, 22) and nowhere else in Genesis. The components are:

- אֵל (El) — the generic Semitic word for deity, the high god of the Canaanite pantheon in pre-Israelite religion, and a legitimate name for the God of Israel throughout the Hebrew Bible.
- עֶלְיוֹן (Elyon, "most high, supreme, uppermost") — from the root עָלָה (alah, "to go up, to ascend"), designating the highest or supreme being.

The epithet El Elyon ("God Most High") appears in the Psalms (7:17, 9:2, 21:7, 46:4, 47:2, 57:2, 78:35, 91:1, 97:9) and in later prophetic material as a recognized title for the God of Israel. Its appearance in the mouth of Melchizedek — a Canaanite-adjacent priest-king of Salem — has generated substantial discussion: is this a genuine identification of Abram's God with a Canaanite high-god? The text's answer is given immediately in verse 22: Abram uses the same title — אֵל עֶלְיוֹן (El Elyon) — in his oath to the king of Sodom, but conjoins it with the covenant name יְהוָה (YHWH): "I have lifted my hand to the LORD, God Most High." Abram identifies YHWH with El Elyon — the God he worships is the God Most High that Melchizedek blesses. The identification is explicit and is Abram's own theological claim.

The phrase קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ (qoneh shamayim va'arets) attached to both occurrences of El Elyon in the blessing and in Abram's oath is one of the most contested clauses in Genesis 14. The verb form קֹנֵה is a Qal participle of the root קָנָה (qanah), which has two distinct but related semantic domains:

(a) **"To acquire, to purchase, to possess"** — used throughout the Hebrew Bible for acquiring property, buying slaves, and possessing goods. On this reading, the phrase means God is the one who acquired or possesses heaven and earth — the proprietor of the cosmos. This is the dominant ancient and traditional interpretation.

(b) **"To create, to beget, to produce"** — the same root appears in Genesis 4:1 (Eve's declaration at Cain's birth: קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת יְהוָה, "I have created/acquired a man with the LORD"), and possibly in Proverbs 8:22 (Wisdom speaking: "The LORD qanani at the beginning of his work"). On this reading, the phrase means God is the one who created heaven and earth — the creator of the cosmos.

The distinction is not minor: "possessor of heaven and earth" emphasizes ownership and dominion; "creator of heaven and earth" emphasizes the act of origination. Both are theologically true affirmations. The LXX renders ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν (hos ektisen ton ouranon kai ten gen, "who created the heaven and the earth") — unambiguously choosing the "create" interpretation, using κτίζω (ktizō, "to create, to found, to establish"), a word with no commercial or acquisitive sense. This is a significant exegetical decision by the LXX translators.

The Vulgate renders qui creavit caelum et terram ("who created heaven and earth") — also following the "create" interpretation.

The formal translation renders "possessor of heaven and earth." This decision rests on three considerations:

First, the immediate literary context of Genesis 14 is saturated with the vocabulary of acquisition and possession: the five kings' possessions (רְכוּשׁ) are taken (v. 11), Lot's possessions (רְכוּשׁ) are taken (v. 12), Abram recovers all the possessions (v. 16), and the king of Sodom and Abram dispute possession of persons and goods (vv. 21–24). The chapter is a sustained meditation on who possesses what, and Melchizedek's blessing declares that the ultimate possessor of all things — heaven and earth — is God Most High. In this context, the "possessor" nuance of qanah is the more contextually resonant reading.

Second, the verbal form קֹנֵה is a present-tense participial — it describes a continuous state ("the one who is possessor") rather than a past event ("who once created"). The "creator" reading better suits a perfect verb (בָּרָא, bara', as in Genesis 1:1) than this participial form.

Third, the Psalms use אֵל עֶלְיוֹן in contexts that emphasize dominion and ownership over the cosmos (Psalm 47:2: "the LORD Most High is awesome, a great king over all the earth"). The contextual field supports "possessor."

The matter is genuinely contested, and the LXX's "created" has ancient warrant. The formal translation notes the LXX variant in the table below and allows readers to weigh the alternatives.

**7. וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ — "And He Blessed Him" (v. 19) — The Direction of Blessing**

The syntactic referent of "he blessed him" in verse 19 requires resolution. In context, Melchizedek is the subject and Abram is the object — "And he [Melchizedek] blessed him [Abram]." This is confirmed by the explicit content of verse 19: "Blessed be Abram by God Most High." The Epistle to the Hebrews (7:7) draws the theological conclusion that this direction of blessing establishes the superiority of Melchizedek's priestly order over the Levitical order, because "it is beyond dispute that the inferior is blessed by the superior." The formal translation allows the text to speak for itself: "he blessed him" with the referents clear from context.

**8. וַיִּתֶּן לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר מִכֹּל — "And Abram Gave Him a Tenth of Everything" (v. 20)**

The subject of the verb "gave" in verse 20 is disputed. The clause וַיִּתֶּן לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר מִכֹּל (vayyitten lo ma'aser mikkol) most naturally reads with Abram as the subject and Melchizedek as the indirect object ("he gave him a tenth of everything") — as the formal translation renders it. Abram's tenth is given to the priest of God Most High. This reading is confirmed by the Epistle to the Hebrews (7:4: "Abraham gave Melchizedek a tenth of the spoils"). The alternative reading — that Melchizedek gave Abram a tenth — lacks contextual support and contradicts the entire tenor of the episode, in which Melchizedek is the giver of blessing and the receiver of honor.

The word מַעֲשֵׂר (ma'aser, "a tenth, a tithe") derives from the root עָשַׂר (asar, "ten"). This is the first occurrence of the concept of tithing in the Hebrew Bible. No law mandating the tithe has yet been given (the Mosaic tithe laws appear in Numbers 18:21–32 and Deuteronomy 14:22–29); Abram's gift of a tenth to the priest-king is a voluntary act, presenting itself as a spontaneous recognition of Melchizedek's priestly authority. The significance of a pre-Mosaic tithe offered to a non-Levitical priest is precisely the point developed in Hebrews 7:1–10.

**9. The Oath Formula — "I Have Lifted My Hand to the LORD, God Most High" (v. 22)**

Abram's refusal of the king of Sodom's offer is framed by a solemn oath formula: הֲרִמֹתִי יָדִי אֶל יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ (harímoti yadi el YHWH El Elyon qoneh shamayim va'arets, "I have lifted my hand to the LORD, God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth"). The gesture of lifting the hand (הֵרִים יָד, herim yad) is the standard ancient oath posture — one raises the hand toward heaven when swearing. It is depicted in Egyptian and Mesopotamian iconography and attested throughout the Hebrew Bible (Exodus 6:8; Deuteronomy 32:40; Ezekiel 20:5–6). The formal translation renders "I have lifted my hand" to preserve both the oath formula and the gestural idiom.

The verse conjoins יְהוָה (YHWH) with אֵל עֶלְיוֹן (El Elyon), making explicit what verse 18 implied: the God Abram worships and the God Melchizedek names are one. This identification of YHWH with El Elyon is Abram's own theological statement, and it is one of the earliest explicit theological affirmations in the Abrahamic narrative. The formal translation renders "the LORD, God Most High" — using "the LORD" for the Tetragrammaton and "God Most High" for El Elyon, conjoined without punctuation that would imply these are two separate beings.

**10. חוּט וּשְׂרוֹךְ נַעַל — "A Thread or a Sandal Strap" (v. 23)**

Abram's oath of total renunciation is expressed through a merism — two complementary objects representing the complete range of trivial possessions:

אִם מִחוּט וְעַד שְׂרוֹךְ נַעַל
"If from a thread even to a sandal strap"

The preposition מִן...וְעַד (min...ve'ad, "from...even to") establishes the merism: from the smallest thread to the most basic article of clothing (a sandal strap). The חוּט (chut, "thread, line") is the most insubstantial textile item; the שְׂרוֹךְ נַעַל (serokh na'al, "sandal strap, shoelace") is the most basic element of footwear. Together they represent: absolutely nothing. Abram will take nothing, from the most worthless to the most ordinary. The formal translation renders "a thread or a sandal strap" — capturing the merism while using the standard English idiom. The LXX renders ἀπὸ σπαρτίου ἕως σφαιρωτῆρος ὑποδήματος (apo spartiou heos sphairotos hypodermatos, "from a cord to the thong of a sandal") — in substantial agreement with the MT.

The stated reason is explicit and remarkable: "lest you should say, 'I have made Abram rich'" (פֶּן תֹּאמַר אֲנִי הֶעֱשַׁרְתִּי אֶת אַבְרָם, pen tomar ani he'esharti et Avram). Abram refuses everything from the king of Sodom because he will not permit Sodom to be the source of his prosperity. His wealth comes from God (cf. Genesis 13:2), and he will not compromise that claim by accepting a gift from a city whose moral character he knows and whose king has just proposed a transaction. The formal translation preserves the exact reported speech of the conditional refusal.

**11. The Exception Clause — "What the Young Men Have Eaten" (v. 24)**

Abram's absolute refusal in verse 23 is carefully qualified in verse 24: he exempts from his renunciation (a) what the young men (הַנְּעָרִים, hane'arim) who accompanied him have already consumed as provisions, and (b) the share of his three allies — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre — who went with him on the campaign. Abram accepts nothing for himself but does not impose his own oath on others. He distinguishes between his personal integrity and the legitimate claims of his allies. The formal translation preserves this distinction: "I will take nothing but what the young men have eaten, and the share of the men who went with me." The verbs render accurately: אֶקַּח (eqqach, "I will take") is negated in verse 23 ("I will not take") and then qualified in verse 24 ("nothing but").

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 14

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 9–10 (GEN_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter marker is visible as "Γ CAP. XIIII" at the left margin of page 9. The following variants from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 14:1 | גּוֹיִם ("Goiim," nations) | ἐθνῶν ("of nations") | "Goiim" | LXX renders as common noun; MT preserved as proper name of the coalition |
| 14:13 | הָעִבְרִי ("the Hebrew") | τῷ περάτῃ ("the crosser / the one from across") | "the Hebrew" | LXX interprets etymologically via the "crossing over" (עָבַר) root; MT conventional rendering retained |
| 14:14 | חֲנִיכִים ("trained men," hapax) | οἰκογενεῖς ("house-born") | "trained men" | LXX renders only the "born in household" aspect; MT's military-training nuance of chanichim preserved |
| 14:18 | לֶחֶם וָיַיִן ("bread and wine") | ἄρτους καὶ οἶνον ("bread and wine") | "bread and wine" | Full agreement |
| 14:19–20 | קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ ("possessor of heaven and earth") | ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν ("who created heaven and earth") | "possessor of heaven and earth" | LXX chooses the "create" interpretation of qanah via κτίζω; MT participial form better supports "possessor"; major crux noted in full above |
| 14:22 | יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן ("the LORD, God Most High") | τῷ θεῷ τῷ ὑψίστῳ ("to God Most High") | "the LORD, God Most High" | LXX omits YHWH, rendering only the El Elyon epithet; MT's explicit conjunction of YHWH with El Elyon preserved — theologically significant |
| 14:23 | שְׂרוֹךְ נַעַל ("sandal strap") | σφαιρωτῆρος ὑποδήματος ("thong of sandal") | "sandal strap" | Full agreement in sense |

The most significant LXX variant is at verse 22: the Vaticanus omits the Tetragrammaton יְהוָה from Abram's oath formula, rendering only "God Most High." The MT explicitly conjoins the covenant name YHWH with the El Elyon epithet — this is Abram's own theological identification of his God. The LXX's omission of YHWH at this point may reflect either a Vorlage that lacked the divine name, a scribal adjustment to harmonize with Melchizedek's non-YHWH formulation in verses 19–20, or the standard LXX practice of sometimes avoiding the Tetragrammaton. In any case, the MT's reading is the more theologically precise: Abram's oath makes explicit that the God he worships as YHWH is the same God Melchizedek invokes as El Elyon. This identification is preserved in the formal translation.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 14 — Three Movements**

Genesis 14 is the most narratively complex chapter in the Patriarchal prologue of Genesis 12–25. It operates on three distinct narrative levels, each framed by the actions of different pairs of actors:

**Movement 1 — The War of Kings (vv. 1–12):** The eastern coalition defeats the five kings of the plain, sacks Sodom and Gomorrah, and takes Lot captive. This section reads like an ancient military chronicle, dense with proper names, geographic markers, and the names of pre-Israelite peoples (Rephaim, Zuzim, Emim, Horites). Its purpose in the narrative is twofold: to establish the world-historical scale of the conflict into which Abram will intervene, and to bring Lot to a point of maximum vulnerability that requires Abram's action. Lot's capture is the pivot of the entire chapter.

**Movement 2 — Abram's Campaign (vv. 13–16):** Abram receives the news, musters his trained retainers, and conducts a swift night raid that defeats the eastern coalition and recovers everything — including Lot. This section is brief but precise: it names Abram's human allies (Mamre, Eshcol, Aner — the same three who will appear in verse 24 and in the Mamre of Genesis 18), specifies the number of Abram's force (318), and identifies the geographic extent of the pursuit (Dan to Hobah, north of Damascus). Abram acts as a military and political force capable of defeating the eastern coalition that had just overthrown five kings. His victory is complete.

**Movement 3 — Two Kings, Two Responses (vv. 17–24):** After the victory, two kings come out to meet Abram. The contrast between them is the theological heart of the chapter:

- Melchizedek, king of Salem, priest of God Most High: comes with bread and wine, blesses Abram by name, blesses God Most High for delivering the enemies, and receives a tenth of everything. His interaction with Abram is entirely a transaction of blessing — the flow of divine favor recognizes and honors Abram's military achievement as an act of the Most High God.

- The king of Sodom: comes with a transactional proposal — give me the persons, keep the goods for yourself. His interaction with Abram is entirely commercial. He offers to make Abram rich by ratifying his ownership of the spoils.

Abram's response to each king is its own theological statement. To Melchizedek, he gives a tenth — a voluntary act of worship and recognition. To the king of Sodom, he refuses everything — a categorical refusal to be enriched by Sodom. The wealth Abram received at the end of the Egypt episode (Genesis 12:16) was given by Pharaoh; the wealth God has promised him (Genesis 13:2, 14–17) comes from the LORD. Abram will not compromise the source of his prosperity by accepting a single thread from a king of a city the narrator has already characterized as exceedingly sinful (Genesis 13:13).

**The Mystery of Melchizedek's Origins**

No genealogy, ancestry, or backstory is given for Melchizedek. He is simply there — king and priest simultaneously, already worshiping God Most High in Salem, already a figure of authority in the land that God has promised to Abram. The Epistle to the Hebrews makes the point that his lack of recorded genealogy is itself theologically meaningful: "without father or mother or genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life, but resembling the Son of God he continues a priest forever" (Hebrews 7:3). Whether or not the author of Hebrews reads a pre-incarnation appearance here (a "Christophany"), the text of Genesis 14 is notably silent where the patriarchal narratives are normally voluble about ancestry and lineage. The silence is eloquent.

**Bread and Wine**

Melchizedek "brought out bread and wine" (וַיֹּצֵא לֶחֶם וָיַיִן, vayyotse lechem vayayin). The simplest reading is that Melchizedek provides provisions for Abram and his exhausted returning warriors — a standard act of royal hospitality in the ancient Near East. But the combination לֶחֶם וָיַיִן (lechem vayayin, "bread and wine") is striking: these are the two primary elements of the ancient Mediterranean meal, and they will become the elements of the Passover table and of the Eucharist. Whether the text intends a proleptic symbolic meaning or a straightforward hospitality gesture, the formal translation renders the plain sense: "bread and wine." No translation decision is made on behalf of the later symbolic freight, which the reader may perceive or not.

**The First Tithe and the Order of Melchizedek**

Abram's gift of a tenth to Melchizedek is the first tithe in the Hebrew Bible. It precedes the Mosaic tithe laws by centuries of narrative time. The Epistle to the Hebrews (7:1–10) develops an elaborate argument from this precedent: because Abram tithed to Melchizedek, and because Levi was "in the loins of Abraham" when the tithe was given, the Levitical priesthood effectively tithed through their ancestor to Melchizedek's priestly order. Therefore the Melchizedekian order is greater than the Levitical. And since Psalm 110:4 ("you are a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek") addresses the Messianic king, the order of the Messiah's priesthood is not Aaronic but Melchizedekian — not dependent on Mosaic law but on a pre-Mosaic, pre-Levitical, divinely ordained precedent from the days of Abram.

The formal translation renders this foundational text with fidelity to the Hebrew without anticipating or foreclosing its later theological development. The text says what it says. The reader and the tradition are left to draw their conclusions.
